figures.caption: Figure 4: We show the empirical distribution of entropy values across the OpenWebText validation commonly used (Pynadath et al., 2025; Sahoo et al., 2025; 2024).

Figure 3: We visualize how single point metrics can be viewed as individual slices of the same underlying frontiers, just evaluated at different points along each curve. Thus generative frontiers provide a unifying framework for interpreting potentially disparate rankings. While single point metrics measure inference settings, frontiers compare generative model capability.

Figure 5: We illustrate how the median entropy and evaluation perplexity of the validation set of OpenWebText can be used to compare dLLMs. We use the AR Eval perplexity reported by Sahoo et al. (2024). We observe that different models excel at different regions – when matching the entropy of natural language, Duo excels. When matching the likelihood of natural language, CANDI excels. This demonstrates that generative quality evaluation is multi-faceted.

Figure 6: We compare the frontiers generated by a 50k checkpoint and a fully trained checkpoint for MDLM, Duo, and CANDI. We note that the partially trained and fully trained checkpoints align fairly closely, suggesting that 50k steps is a reasonable budget when iterating on method design.

Table 1: Sample entropy values for each method across NFE budgets under three temperature settings. As shown, all entropies for the temperature settings are reasonable – there is no evidence of diversity hacking that leads to degenerate outputs. Yet even these minor entropy differences are sufficient to reverse rankings.

Figure 2: We demonstrate why single point evaluations are inherently ambiguous. Even though point B may have a better perplexity, plotting out the frontiers demonstrates that point A actually is closer to the target distribution. Only when points are matched on entropy or perplexity can meaningful conclusions be drawn about distance to the reference distribution.
